content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 31, 2010

Joseph A. Medrano
Clayton City Councilman
807 Savignon Court
Clayton, California 94517

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-204

Dear Mr. Medrano:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

As a member of the Clayton City Council may you participate in a governmental decision involving a project that will come before the City Council, when that project involves real property located within 500 feet of the location of your business, where you hold a month-to-month periodic tenancy?

CONCLUSION

Yes. Because you do not have any economic interests that would be affected by the governmental decision, you may participate in the governmental decision.

FACTS

You are a Clayton City Councilman. There is a project that will be coming before the City Council in 2011. You have an office in downtown Clayton within your redevelopment district but beyond 500 feet of the project that will come before the council. Part of the plan may consider waiving parking requirements, which would allow for additional parking within 500 feet of your office. You do not have a leasehold interest in the property where you run your council.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-10-204
Page No. 2

business, and you are on a month-to-month periodic tenancy basis for the rent. The rent is $400
per month. You state that the parking consideration would not impact your business negatively
or positively.

You are requesting an opinion on whether or not you must recuse yourself from
participating in discussion and voting on this project based on the location of your office.

ANALYSIS
POTENTIAL CONFLICT OF INTEREST

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.

The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a governmental
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her
financial interests.

STEPS 1 & 2: ARE YOU A PUBLIC OFFICIAL MAKING, PARTICIPATING IN MAKING, OR
INFLUENCING A GOVERNMENTAL DECISION?

As a Clayton City Councilman you are a public official under the Act. (Section 82048.)
Consequently, you may not make, participate in making, or otherwise use your official position to
influence any decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of
your economic interests. Because you will be asked to make decisions regarding a new project
you will be making, participating in making, or otherwise using your official position to influence
a governmental decision.

STEP 3: DO YOU HAVE POTENTIALLY DISQUALIFYING ECONOMIC INTERESTS?

A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section
87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests, including:

• An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a));
or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b).)

File No. A-10-204
Page No. 3

• An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)

• An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)

• An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
$420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e);
Regulation 18703.4.)

• An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18703.5.)

You have stated that you own a business located within 500 feet of some parking that
might be affected by the governmental decision. You have already concluded that there will be
no impact positively or negatively on your business interest as a result of the governmental
decisions that would result in a conflict of interest. Therefore, our analyses is limited to the
interest in the real property, if any, upon which your office is located.

You also stated that you do not have a lease for the real property where you run your
business and instead you have a month-to-month periodic tenancy. A month-to-month tenancy is
not considered to be an economic interest under the Act. (Regulation 18233.)

Accordingly, you have not identified any economic interests that would be impacted by
the governmental decision involving the project,² and therefore you do not have a conflict of
interest with respect to the governmental decision based upon the facts you have provided.
Please contact us for further advice if you believe that your personal finances or your business
will be impacted by the decisions on the project in any way.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Sukhi K. Brar
Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl

² Accepting your representation that the governmental decisions regarding the parking will have no
financial impact on your business.

QUESTION: As a member of the Clayton City Council may you participate in a governmental decision
involving a project that will come before the City Council, when that project involves real
property located within 500 feet of the location of your business, where you hold a month-to-
month periodic tenancy?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Because you do not have any economic interests that would be affected by the
governmental decision, you may participate in the governmental decision.
